The centerpiece is a whole roasted haunch—slow-cooked over low coals for twelve hours, basted with rendered fat and wild herb oil, until the outside is crisp and caramelized and the inside falls apart at the touch of a fork. Around it, roasted root vegetables from the greenhouse: carrots, turnips, parsnips, golden and sweet. Fresh bread from the new oven, crusty and warm, the crumb soft and steamy. And biscuits—my recipe, the one that started it all—flaky layers of flour and rendered fat, pulled apart by hand and eaten hot. There's sausage, too. Batch twelve, perfected—sage and garlic and a hint of the wild onion Rosa found by the creek. There's a stew, because there's always stew, rich and dark and full of tender chunks. There's preserved fruit from the summer—plums and blackberries, tart and sweet, spooned over biscuits for dessert. And there's something new. Something I've been working on for weeks. A rendered fat toffee—slow-cooked with the last of the honey from a wild hive Sol found, poured thin and left to harden. It snaps when you bite it, then melts on the tongue, rich and buttery and sweet. Cass watched me make it and said nothing. She knows what the fat is. She eats it anyway. --- The dining room is full. Twenty-six people crowded around the long tables, candles burning, the room warm from bodies and the big wood stove in the corner. Jude stands at the head of the table. He has a cup of the potato liquor. We all do. "One year," he says. His voice is steady, as always. No emotion. Just facts. "We made it one year. Not because we're lucky. Not because we're special. Because we worked. Because we made hard choices. Because we fed each other." He raises his cup. We raise ours. "To survival," he says. "To survival," we echo. We drink. The liquor burns. We eat. The noise rises—conversation, laughter, the clatter of forks on plates. Beck is already on his second helping of the haunch, juice running down his chin, grinning. Rosa is eating the sausage without complaint, which from Rosa is a standing ovation. The newcomers eat with the desperate focus of people who remember starvation, eyes wide at the abundance, at the flavor, at the simple fact of fullness. I eat standing up, by the stove, in my kitchen. A slice of the haunch, tender enough to cut with a spoon, smoky and deep. A biscuit, torn and dripping with fat. A piece of the toffee, which melts on my tongue like a secret. It's good. God help me, it's good. The best food I've ever made. The best food I've ever eaten. A year of practice, of refinement, of learning the ingredient—its texture, its fat content, the way it responds to heat, the way it renders and crisps and falls apart. I know this ingredient better than I know myself. I know exactly what it needs. Cass comes into the kitchen. She's flushed from the heat, from the food, from the potato liquor. She's been eating. She's been part of this for months now. "They love it," she says. "Good." "The toffee is—God, Maren. The toffee." "I know." She leans against the counter beside me. We listen to the noise from the dining room—the sounds of people eating, talking, living. It's warm. It's good. It's everything we built. "Happy anniversary," she says. I almost smile. "Same to you." She goes back to the dining room. I stay in the kitchen. I eat another piece of toffee. I listen. --- Later, after the plates are cleared and the candles are guttering and the settlement sleeps, I'm alone in the kitchen. Washing the pots. Drying the knives. Banking the fire. Routine. Ritual. The work that keeps the dark place sealed. I find it on the cutting board, half-hidden under a pile of vegetable scraps. A drawing. Crumpled, stained with sap, fused slightly to a scrap of chitin-plate that must have come off one of the carcasses this morning. I unfold it. Crayon on paper. A house, lopsided, with a door and two windows and a chimney with smoke coming out. In front of the house, two stick figures—one big, one small, holding hands. Above them, in shaky, childish letters: "MY FAMLY." I stare at it for a long time. I don't know which carcass it came from. I don't know when it was swallowed, or by what, or why. I don't know if the thing that swallowed it was once a child who drew pictures, or a parent who kept them, or something else entirely. Something that found the drawing and absorbed it the way it absorbs everything else, through the skin, without thought, without meaning. I fold the drawing. I put it in the jar. The jar is on the top shelf, behind the flour sacks, where nobody looks. It's full now. Forty, maybe fifty objects. A button. A zipper pull. A fragment of fabric that might have been a shirt. The things that don't burn, don't render, don't break down. The leftovers. The evidence. I close the jar. I put it back on the shelf. I wipe my hands on my apron. From the pens, the hum. Faint. Thinning. The herd is smaller than it was a year ago. We've eaten well. We've eaten too well. The supply isn't infinite. Nothing is infinite. I look out the kitchen window. The tree line is dark, barely visible against the sky. The spores drift through the air like snow, catching the moonlight, beautiful if you don't think about what they are. I think about tomorrow's menu. The haunch will be gone by morning—Beck will see to that. The sausage is running low. The vegetables are holding. The biscuits—I have enough flour for a week, maybe two. I think about the herd. The thinning herd. The hunting parties that go farther every time. The spores that get thicker the deeper you go. The coughing that lasts longer each trip. I think about the jar on the shelf. The drawing. The house with the smoke. The stick figures holding hands. "MY FAMLY." I think about the toffee, melting on my tongue. The buttery sweetness. The richness. The way it coats your throat and warms your chest and makes you close your eyes and forget, just for a second, what it is. I think about Cass, asleep in the dining room, full and warm and part of this now. I think about Jude, in his office, running the numbers. I think about Sol, wherever Sol goes at night, doing whatever Sol does. I think about Beck, dreaming about breakfast. I think about the hum. Tomorrow I'll wake up. I'll start the fire. I'll check the curing shed. I'll begin the prep for whatever the hunting party brings in. I'll make biscuits. I'll make stew. I'll feed them. I'll watch them eat. I'll be proud of what I made. And at night, when the kitchen is quiet and the hum is all that's left, I'll think about the jar on the shelf and the drawing inside and the question I can't answer and won't ask. But that's tomorrow. Tonight, the kitchen is warm. The fire is banked. The knives are sharp. I bank the fire. I lie on my cot. The hum continues. I let it. I sleep.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 8, the final chapter of the story, and it functions as a culmination of the narrative's central themes: the banality of atrocity, the erosion of moral resistance, and the quiet horror of survival. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. **Craft and Prose Quality** The prose is largely consistent with the established voice—utilitarian, sensory, grounded in the physical work of cooking. The writing avoids the purple prose trap for the most part. Phrases like "the work that keeps the dark place sealed" and "Language is a choice" are economical and effective. The description of the Founding Feast is genuinely evocative without becoming overwrought. The toffee as a symbol of refinement and normalization works well. However, there are moments where the prose reaches for significance in ways that feel slightly calculated rather than earned. The repetition of "I think about..." in the final section is a structural device that works in moderation but is deployed perhaps one beat too many times, tipping from meditative into slightly mechanical. **The Jar and the Drawing** This is the chapter's central creative decision, and it's a significant one. The plan explicitly stated: "No finding children's toys in the stomachs." The jar of objects was not in the chapter plan at all—it appears to be an addition. The drawing ("MY FAMLY") is dangerously close to the sentimentality the writing plan explicitly forbade. The plan said: "No finding children's toys in the stomachs." A child's crayon drawing of "MY FAMLY" found in a carcass is almost exactly that—the kind of humanizing artifact the plan was designed to avoid. This is a significant deviation from the stated intention. The plan's horror was meant to come from the *absence* of such moments—from the fact that there is no moral anchor, no humanizing detail that forces a reckoning. The drawing does exactly what the plan said not to do: it provides a moment of potential moral weight, a suggestion that these creatures might have been human once, that they kept children's drawings. The jar of objects compounds this—it's a collection of evidence of humanity, which is precisely the kind of sentimentality the plan rejected. To the chapter's credit, Maren doesn't break down. She folds the drawing, puts it in the jar, and goes to sleep. The horror is in her *not* reacting. But the drawing itself is still there, doing the work of humanization that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. It's a compromise—the author wants the emotional resonance of the humanizing detail while maintaining Maren's numbness. This is somewhat effective but also somewhat contradictory to the stated vision. **Character Consistency** Maren is largely consistent with her profile. The cooking-as-avoidance, the pride in craft, the emotional translation into physical sensation—all present. The "almost smile" at Cass's "Happy anniversary" is exactly right. Her staying in the kitchen while others celebrate is perfect. Cass's arc is handled well. "She knows what the fat is. She eats it anyway" is a strong, efficient line. Her enthusiasm about the toffee ("God, Maren. The toffee.") shows her assimilation without belaboring it. Jude's toast is perfectly in character—"Not because we're lucky. Not because we're special. Because we worked." Efficient, unsentimental, exactly right. Sol is absent from this chapter, which is a missed opportunity given Sol's role in the hunting party and the spore exposure. **The Chapter Plan vs. Execution** The plan for Chapter 8 called for: Maren feeling strange from spore exposure, her skin looking pale/grey, a craving for something raw and earthy, looking at the carcass and seeing "something else," and ending with her taking a bite of raw meat that "tastes like home." The ambiguity about whether she's turning. The actual chapter delivers almost none of this. Instead, it's a Founding Feast chapter—a celebration, an anniversary meal, a moment of community warmth. The spore infection angle is mentioned only in retrospect ("three weeks ago"). The transformation arc is entirely absent. The ending is Maren going to sleep, not biting raw meat. The chapter plan's ambiguous, disturbing ending—the suggestion of Maren's own transformation—is replaced with a more conventional "quiet horror of the jar" ending. This is a substantial deviation from the plan. The plan's Chapter 8 was meant to be the most disturbing chapter, the one that closes the loop on the horror by suggesting Maren herself is becoming what she eats. Instead, the chapter delivers a cozy-horror feast scene with a humanizing artifact that the plan explicitly forbade. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse and functional, which suits the story. "They love it." / "Good." / "The toffee is—God, Maren. The toffee." / "I know." This is well-handled—economical, character-consistent, not over-explained. Jude's toast is the longest speech and it's appropriately terse. No dialogue feels forced or expository. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally effective in a muted way. The feast scene creates genuine warmth that is undercut by what we know. The jar is a strong image even if it violates the plan. The final sequence—the hum, the sleep, the routine—lands with quiet dread. The toffee as the chapter's recurring sensory anchor is well-chosen. **The "Cozy Horror" Balance** This chapter actually delivers the cozy-horror balance better than the plan's Chapter 8 would have. The feast is genuinely warm and communal; the horror is in the subtext, in the jar, in the thinning herd, in the spores drifting like snow. The line "beautiful if you don't think about what they are" is the chapter's thesis, and it works. **Overall Assessment** This is a competent, often effective final chapter that deviates significantly from its stated plan. The prose is clean and controlled. The character work is mostly strong. The emotional register is appropriate. But the jar/drawing sequence is a meaningful compromise of the story's stated principles, and the absence of the transformation arc (the most interesting element of the plan) is a significant missed opportunity. The chapter delivers a satisfying conclusion but not the disturbing, ambiguous one the plan promised. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 5 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a remarkably accomplished piece of post-apocalyptic horror-adjacent fiction that takes the prompt's darkly comic premise—eating the monsters—and transforms it into something genuinely unsettling and morally complex. Let me assess it carefully across all dimensions. STRENGTHS: The narrative voice is the piece's greatest achievement. Maren's first-person perspective is consistent, controlled, and deeply inhabited. The cook's professional detachment—the way she thinks in terms of cuts, ratios, and techniques—functions as both characterization and psychological defense mechanism. This is shown rather than told with remarkable consistency. The line "Language is a choice. You choose the words that let you sleep" is earned, not decorative. The world-building is economical and effective. The Oysters are genuinely unsettling creatures—eyeless, humming, with vestigial hands—and the story wisely never fully resolves whether they were once human. The jar on the shelf, accumulating artifacts (the crayon drawing of "MY FAMLY" being the most devastating), is a masterful recurring motif that does enormous thematic work without being heavy-handed. The cozy-horror tonal balance is well-executed. The warmth of the kitchen, the satisfaction of good food, the community gathering—these genuinely cozy elements are in constant, productive tension with the horror of what's being cooked. The story earns its darkness by making the comfort real first. Character differentiation is strong. Sol's robotic efficiency, Jude's clipboard-and-numbers pragmatism, Beck's appetite, Rosa's pickiness—these are economical but effective. Cass functions as the moral conscience and reader surrogate, and her arc from discovery to compromise is the story's emotional spine. The pacing across eight chapters is generally well-managed. The story builds from establishment (Chapters 1-2) through complication (Chapters 3-4) to moral crisis (Chapters 5-6) and then a kind of bittersweet resolution (Chapters 7-8). The anniversary feast in Chapter 8 is a genuinely effective culmination. WEAKNESSES: The repetition of certain phrases and structures becomes noticeable. "Cold, I tell myself. Just cold" appears twice. The "dark place" metaphor is used multiple times. The ending structure—"I bank the fire. I lie on my cot. The hum continues."—is repeated almost verbatim across multiple chapters, which initially feels like intentional rhythm but eventually feels like a crutch. By Chapter 8, the reader has seen this closing pattern five or six times and it has lost its power. Cass's transformation in Chapter 6 is the piece's weakest moment. Her capitulation after four days of starvation is believable on a survival level, but the emotional resolution—"I'm in. Not because you're right. Because I'm hungry"—feels slightly too neat. The conversation in her room where Maren confesses to being proud of her cooking is the story's most on-the-nose moment; Maren's voice cracks and she presses her palms against her eyes, which is the most melodramatic the story gets and feels slightly unearned given her established stoicism. The "what if they were people" question is raised repeatedly but never developed beyond its initial framing. The crayon drawing in Chapter 8 is the story's most powerful image, but it arrives late and the story ends before fully reckoning with it. The moral ambiguity is preserved, which is the right choice, but the story sometimes uses that ambiguity as a way to avoid committing to anything. Sol is underused as a character. The robotic efficiency is established but never complicated or deepened. Sol's line "The processing time varies. Some adapt in days. Some take weeks. Some don't adapt" is the story's most chilling moment, but Sol remains a functional device rather than a character. Some of the "poetic" observations land well ("They don't bleed red. It's more like sap, thick and amber, and it smells sweet when it hits the air. Then it sours. Everything sours eventually") but others are slightly forced ("like pages in a book" for biscuit layers, the repeated refrigerator metaphor which is effective the first time but overused). The dialogue is generally functional but occasionally expository. Cass's speeches in Chapter 5 about empathy and teaching feel slightly like the author making an argument rather than a character speaking. The "cozy" element of the prompt is well-served—the kitchen warmth, the biscuits, the community meals—but the "horror" element is more psychological dread than actual horror, which is a legitimate interpretation but means the piece never quite delivers a visceral shock. Overall, this is a sophisticated, well-crafted piece that demonstrates genuine literary ambition and mostly achieves it. The voice is distinctive, the moral complexity is real, and the best moments (the jar, the drawing, the first stew) are genuinely memorable. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 15 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Unearned Transformations: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 74.7/100
+ Victorian Romance — The Upper Window (Chapter Avg: 77.5 | Final: 77.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Initial prompt interpretation:** The phrase "one quickly learns" suggests this is a lesson learned through necessity — perhaps by a protagonist who didn't start as a thief or burglar but found themselves needing this skill. The Victorian setting implies gaslight, rigid social hierarchies, corsetry, and perhaps the contrast between gleaming drawing rooms and shadowy servant corridors. The "upper windows" detail is specific and evocative — suggesting that the ground floor is impenetrable, but the upper stories offer opportunity. Why? Perhaps the ground floor is too well-guarded, or perhaps the person entering is coming from above — rooftops, adjoining buildings, trees? **Character directions:** 1. **A governess or lady's companion** — someone with legitimate access to a manor's upper floors who discovers she can use that access for something else. Perhaps she's been wronged and is gathering evidence, or perhaps she's helping someone else. 2. **A jewel thief** — perhaps female, perhaps a widow or fallen gentlewoman forced into crime. Romantic tension with the manor's owner or a detective. Could be played as a caper with heart, or as genuinely desperate. 3. **A journalist or pamphleteer** — someone breaking in to expose corruption, a secret, or a scandal. Perhaps writing under a pseudonym. The "upper windows" are literal — climbing the exterior. 4. **A young woman escaping** — perhaps from a cruel guardian or an arranged marriage, using the windows to come and go from her own supposed home, eventually escaping entirely. 5. **A smuggler or courier** — perhaps helping people (escaped convicts, political refugees, lovers eloping) move through the countryside using manor houses as waypoints. The upper windows are routes through places where she has no official access. 6. **A romantic rival or estranged family member** — entering to confront, claim an inheritance, or sabotage. The window entry is born of not being welcome through the front door. **Romantic directions:** - **Forbidden romance between classes** — thief and lord, servant and master, widow and bachelor. - **Enemies to lovers** — the person catching her is someone she clashes with, perhaps the manor's owner, his brother, or a detective investigating her. - **Second chance romance** — she knew him before she fell, and entering his manor through the window is both literal and metaphorical. - **Slow burn with tension** — every encounter is charged with the dual danger of exposure and attraction. **Plot structures:** 1. **Caper structure** — she's planning something specific (a theft, an investigation, an escape) and the story builds toward it. Each chapter is a step in the plan, with escalating complications and romantic entanglement. 2. **Mystery structure** — there's a secret in the manor, and each window entry reveals another piece. The romance develops alongside the mystery. 3. **Crisis structure** — something forces her into the manor repeatedly (a trapped person, evidence she needs, items she's been selling that must be returned). Each entry is higher stakes. 4. **Slow revelation structure** — the reader doesn't fully understand her purpose until late in the story. Each chapter peels back a layer. **Tonal directions:** - **Gothic and atmospheric** — fog, gaslight, shadows, heavy curtains, old secrets. Think du Maurier, Brontë. - **Witty and spirited** — banter, comedic near-misses, the romance crackling with intelligence. Think Heyer. - **Melancholy and tender** — longing, loss, the ache of wanting something you can't have. Think Hardy. - **Adventurous and bold** — the thrill of the climb, the danger of discovery, exhilaration. Think Stevenson meets Gaskell. **Key imagery and details to explore:** - The physicality of climbing — gloves, skirts, the practicality of dress in Victorian England. How *does* one climb in a corset? - The contrast between the exterior (cold, dangerous, exposed) and interior (warm, candlelit, suffocating in its propriety). - Windows as thresholds between worlds — public and private, respectable and scandalous, safety and danger. - The architecture of Victorian manors — multiple stories, ivy or trellises, balconies, dormers, servants' quarters on upper floors. - Moonlight, candlelight, the quality of light when you shouldn't be somewhere. **Possible chapter breakdown ideas (loose):** - **Chapter 1:** Establish the skill — she enters somewhere through a window. Establish who she is and why this is necessary. Perhaps a close call. - **Chapter 2-3:** The manor is introduced. Why this manor? What does she need there? Early encounters, perhaps with servants, perhaps with the man of the house. - **Chapter 4-5:** Escalation. The romance or relationship develops. The task becomes more complicated. She's almost caught. - **Chapter 6:** Crisis or reversal. Something goes wrong — she's discovered, or she discovers something she didn't expect. - **Chapter 7:** The stakes are highest. A final entry into the manor, or a final confrontation. - **Chapter 8:** Resolution — romantic and plot. What happens when the window is no longer needed? --- # Reflection Several threads here feel promising. The strongest instinct I have is that the story works best when **the "one" learning this skill is someone who never expected to become a person who enters through upper windows** — not a professional thief, but someone thrust into it. That creates inherent tension and character depth: she's resourceful but not hardened, brave but frightened, skilled but not invincible. The governess/lady's companion angle has a lot of structural advantages. It gives her legitimate reason to be *inside* a manor, which means the story can play with the contrast between being invited in and breaking in. But I worry it might feel too familiar — the governess in the big house is well-trod ground in Victorian romance. The **widow or fallen gentlewoman** appeals to me more. There's built-in backstory, built-in stakes (what caused her fall? is she trying to reclaim something?), and built-in class tension. If she was once *of* the world she now breaks into, that's poignant. She knows how these houses work — she grew up in one — but now she enters through the servant's window or climbs the ivy. The jewel thief angle could work but risks becoming either too lighthearted (a romp) or too grim (she's desperate and it's sad). I think the sweet spot is **she's stealing something specific, for a specific reason, not out of general criminality** — perhaps something that was stolen from her family, or something she needs to prevent a worse outcome. This gives her moral weight and makes the romantic stakes higher: if the man she's falling for discovers what she's doing, it's not just embarrassment — it's ruin. The romantic pairing with the manor's owner is the obvious choice but has real potential if handled carefully. The dramatic irony of him not knowing who she is (or gradually discovering it) is rich. But I'm also drawn to the idea of the **younger brother or a visiting friend** — someone with less power in the house, which mirrors her position as an outsider. Or even a detective or investigator, if we want more direct conflict. For tonal direction, I think **gothic with warmth** is the right balance. The atmosphere should be rich and moody — fog, candlelight, the weight of old houses — but the romance should cut through that with genuine tenderness and wit. Not full Brontë darkness, but not pure Heyer froth either. Somewhere in the territory of Sarah Waters meets Mary Balogh — serious stakes, beautiful prose, emotional depth. The eight-chapter structure should probably follow **rising action with a single major reversal** rather than trying to twist multiple times. In 8,000 words, there's room for complexity but not for sprawling subplots. One task, one manor, one man, one secret — revealed and resolved. I'm drawn to the idea that the story opens *in media res* with her already climbing — we learn the skill and the stakes simultaneously — and that the final chapter involves her entering (or choosing not to enter) through the front door. The arc from window to door feels like the right metaphorical shape for a romance: from transgression to belonging. The thing I want to avoid is making her a passive romantic object. She should be the active agent — making choices, taking risks, driving the plot. The man she falls for should be interesting and worthy, but the story should be *hers*. She's the one who climbs.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **The Story in a Nutshell:** A fallen gentlewoman, now living in reduced circumstances, has become a discreet "retriever" of items that wealthy clients wish to reclaim from those who have wronged them. She is hired to steal back a specific, scandalous set of letters from a remote Yorkshire manor. Upon arrival, she finds the house occupied not by the villain she expected, but by a reclusive, scholarly younger son who has no idea of the letters' existence — or the true nature of the man whose house he now keeps. She must retrieve the letters without destroying the fragile, unexpected connection forming between them, all while the winter weather and a suspicious butler close in. **Character & Voice:** - **Protagonist (Eleanor "Nell" Carroway):** Late 20s. Once a clergyman's daughter with prospects, now living in a Bloomsbury boarding house. Resourceful, pragmatic, with a core of romanticism she tries to suppress. She narrates in a first-person voice that is wry, observational, and self-aware, but with an undercurrent of longing for a life she can no longer have. - **Love Interest (Mr. Julian Ashworth):** Early 30s. A botanist and amateur astronomer, the second son who inherited the manor unexpectedly after his elder brother's mysterious death abroad. He is quiet, thoughtful, and more at ease with plants and stars than with society. He is not a typical brooding lord, but a gentle, somewhat awkward intellectual. - **Antagonist (The Butler, Mr. Crane):** The true obstacle. Loyal to the *memory* of the elder Ashworth brother, he is suspicious of Nell's arrival and vigilant in protecting the house's secrets, which he may partially know. **Plot & Structure:** - **Arc:** From transactional intrusion to reluctant connection to chosen vulnerability. The "window" motif evolves from literal (climbing) to metaphorical (opening a heart). - **Pacing:** Slow-burn romance interwound with a heist. Each chapter escalates both the intimacy and the danger of discovery. - **Setting:** Ashworth Grange, a crumbling but beautiful Georgian manor in the Yorkshire Dales, late autumn. The atmosphere is key: fog, frost, decaying grandeur, a library full of old books, an observatory tower. **Stylistic Choices:** - **Tone:** Gothic-tinged romance. Atmospheric, with rich sensory detail (the smell of old paper, beeswax, and damp earth; the sound of rain on glass). The romance is tender and intellectual, built on shared curiosity and quiet moments. - **Voice:** First-person, past tense. Nell’s narration is her primary tool for control in a situation where she has little. It should feel intimate, as if she’s confessing to the reader. - **Avoid:** Melodrama, instalove, a villain who is cartoonishly evil. The tension should come from moral ambiguity and emotional stakes, not mustache-twirling. Nell must be competent and proactive, not rescued. **Core Intentions:** 1. Make the reader feel the physical reality of entering through a window — the strain, the fear, the exhilaration. 2. Build a romance that feels earned through specific, small moments of connection (over a shared book, a telescope view, a practical kindness). 3. Use the mystery of the letters to slowly reveal the true darkness of the man Nell thought she was stealing from, making her mission morally complex. 4. End with a choice that redefines what "home" and "safety" mean — perhaps the front door is opened from the inside. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Lesson** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Opening:** *In media res*. Nell is halfway up a rose trellis in the dark, rain making the stone slick. She’s entering the home of a *different* client, establishing her skill and profession. We see her method: observation, timing, practical tools (gloves, a small kit, sturdy boots under her skirt). - **Flashback/Calm:** The next day, in her bleak Bloomsbury room, she is hired by a distraught gentlewoman. The task: retrieve a packet of love letters from Ashworth Grange, written by the client to the late elder Ashworth brother. They could ruin her if published. Payment will secure Nell’s independence for a year. - **Preparation:** Nell researches the house (via an old architectural guide), plans her approach. She learns the younger brother, a recluse, now lives there. She secures a position as a temporary librarian/cataloguer (forged references) to gain legitimate entry, planning to do the real work at night. - **Arrival:** She arrives at the isolated Grange. Descrip tion of its imposing, slightly neglected state. She meets the taciturn butler, Mr. Crane, whose eyes miss nothing. She is shown to her room — an attic chamber with a window that has a very useful view of the roofline. **Chapter 2: The House and Its Ghosts** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Settling In:** Nell begins her "work" in the vast, disordered library. The atmosphere is heavy with the presence of the dead brother — his hunting trophies on the wall, his taste in decor. Crane patrols the halls. - **First Window (Practice):** That night, she does a practice run. She exits her attic window onto the roof, locates the dead brother’s study (on the second floor, now locked). She notes the window latch type, the drop to the garden. It’s risky, doable. - **First Encounter:** Returning along the roof, she is startled by a light in the observatory tower. Julian Ashworth is there, looking through a telescope. He doesn’t see her, but she watches him — a solitary, absorbed figure. He seems harmless, at odds with the house’s oppressive legacy. - **Complication:** The next day, while cataloguing, she finds a hidden drawer in a desk. It’s empty, but *recently* cleaned. Someone else is looking for something here. Her mission just became less straightforward. **Chapter 3: The Astronomer** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Forced Proximity:** A storm rolls in, cutting off the roads. Nell is effectively trapped at the Grange. Julian, as host, is forced to be more present. Their first real conversations happen over books in the library — he is awkward but brilliant, passionate about botany and the cosmos. - **Shared Moment:** He shows her the observatory. The intimacy of the dark, the shared awe of looking at Jupiter’s moons. He speaks of his brother with a complicated mix of grief and relief. "He owned the world. I only wanted to observe it." - **Suspicion:** Crane witnesses their easy(ish) conversation and his suspicion deepens. He finds an excuse to search Nell’s attic room while she’s at dinner. She returns to find her things subtly disturbed. The clock is ticking. - **The Plan Advances:** That night, she makes her first real attempt on the study window. She gets it open, slips inside. The room is a shrine to the dead brother. She finds a locked strongbox in the desk. No letters yet. But as she leaves, she sees a light moving in the garden below — Crane, on a late patrol. **Chapter 4: The Letters and the Ledger** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Discovery:** In the library, Nell follows a hunch and finds not the letters, but a private ledger tucked inside a book on estate management. It details payments to "a fixer" and mentions "the Devon matter" — alluding to a scandal that ruined a family. This was the elder brother’s true nature: cruel, exploitative. - **Moral Shift:** Nell’s mission is no longer just a job. The letters aren’t just embarrassing; they are potential *evidence* of a man’s predatory behavior. Her client may be one of many victims. - **Connection Deepens:** She and Julian bond over a neglected plant in the conservatory. He speaks of his brother’s lack of care for living things. There’s a charged moment where their hands touch over a pot of soil. The line between her role and her feelings blurs dangerously. - **Second Window:** She returns to the study, strongbox in sight. This time, she picks the lock. Inside: not letters, but a packet of *photographs* — compromising, cruel, depicting several women. And a single, damning letter of blackmail. This is worse than she imagined. **Chapter 5: The Storm Inside** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Crisis of Conscience:** Nell is in agony. She has the evidence, but it’s not the specific letters she was hired for. Taking this could expose not just the elder brother’s villainy, but potentially ruin innocent people in the photographs. Her client’s letter is there, but so are others. - **Emotional Climax:** A second, fiercer storm hits. Power fails. In the candlelit library, Julian confesses he feels a profound guilt for inheriting a house built on his brother’s cruelties, which he is only beginning to uncover. He asks Nell, almost desperately, if she believes a place can be redeemed. Their foreheads almost touch in the dark. - **Discovery:** Crane, having found a jemmy in Nell’s room (a tool from her kit), confronts her in the hall. He accuses her of being a thief. Julian intervenes, defending her, creating a rift between the two men. Nell is now under open suspicion. - **Decision:** She must act. She decides to take only her client’s specific letter from the packet and leave the rest, hoping to minimize the damage. But as she prepares for her final window entry, she finds the study door unlocked… and Julian’s lamp inside. **Chapter 6: The Observer Observed** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Confrontation:** Julian is not angry. He is devastated. He found the strongbox days ago. He was waiting to see what *she* would do. He read the letters, saw the photographs. He knows she was hired. "You came through the window," he says, "to steal back a ghost’s shame." - **Truth-Telling:** Nell, cornered, tells him everything: her fall from society, her work, her client’s fear. She explains she only wanted the one letter. Julian reveals his own truth: he’s been trying to find a way to make restitution to the victims, but doesn’t know how. He feels paralyzed by the legacy. - **Shift in Mission:** They form a wary alliance. The goal is no longer theft, but managed exposure. They will retrieve the entire packet of evidence together, with a plan to anonymously return the personal letters to the women, while using the financial records to force the family solicitor to create a compensation fund. - **The Final Window:** Together, they go to the study. But Crane, who has listened at the door, locks them in. He declares he will protect the family name from this "conspiracy." They are trapped, and the evidence is now in Crane’s hands. **Chapter 7: The Open Door** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Escape:** Using Nell’s tools and Julian’s knowledge of the house’s old servant passages, they escape the study. The chase through the dark, quiet house is tense but cooperative — a physical metaphor for their partnership. - **Confrontation with Crane:** They corner Crane in the great hall. Julian, finding his voice, delivers a quiet, devastating speech about what true loyalty means — not to a dead man’s sins, but to justice and the living. He dismisses Crane on the spot. - **The Choice:** With the packet recovered, Nell holds her client’s letter. She could simply take it and leave, completing her contract. But she looks at Julian, at the evidence of other ruined lives. She makes a choice. She puts the entire packet on the table. "We do this properly. All of it." - **Symbolic Action:** As dawn breaks, Julian doesn’t go to his telescope. He goes to the front door, unlocks it, and throws it wide open to the cold, clean morning air. "No more secrets in the dark," he says. **Chapter 8: The Morning Light** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Aftermath:** Weeks later. Nell is back in London, having forwarded the evidence to a trusted solicitor. Her client’s letter was returned; the woman is safe. A quiet financial settlement has been arranged for others. The scandal is contained, but the poison is drained. - **Return:** She receives a simple, botanical drawing from Julian — a rare Yorkshire flower, with a note: *"It blooms in harsh conditions. I should like to show you where. The front door is open. — J.A."* - **Final Scene:** She arrives at Ashworth Grange. It is transformed — Crane gone, windows cleaned, the oppressive atmosphere lifting. Julian greets her not in the library or observatory, but in the garden. He shows her the flower. They talk not of windows or walls, but of seasons, growth, and future plans for the estate. - **Closing Image:** That evening, she walks up the stairs to her old attic room. She opens the window — not to climb out, but to let the night air in. She looks out at the familiar roofline, now just a view, not a path. She closes it gently and goes back downstairs, where light and conversation wait. The art of entering has been forgotten; the art of staying has just begun.</think># Intention **The Story in a Nutshell:** A fallen gentlewoman, now living in reduced circumstances, has become a discreet "retriever" of items that wealthy clients wish to reclaim from those who have wronged them. She is hired to steal back a specific, scandalous set of letters from a remote Yorkshire manor. Upon arrival, she finds the house occupied not by the villain she expected, but by a reclusive, scholarly younger son who has no idea of the letters' existence — or the true nature of the man whose house he now keeps. She must retrieve the letters without destroying the fragile, unexpected connection forming between them, all while the winter weather and a suspicious butler close in. **Character & Voice:** - **Protagonist (Eleanor "Nell" Carroway):** Late 20s. Once a clergyman's daughter with prospects, now living in a Bloomsbury boarding house. Resourceful, pragmatic, with a core of romanticism she tries to suppress. She narrates in a first-person voice that is wry, observational, and self-aware, but with an undercurrent of longing for a life she can no longer have. - **Love Interest (Mr. Julian Ashworth):** Early 30s. A botanist and amateur astronomer, the second son who inherited the manor unexpectedly after his elder brother's mysterious death abroad. He is quiet, thoughtful, and more at ease with plants and stars than with society. He is not a typical brooding lord, but a gentle, somewhat awkward intellectual. - **Antagonist (The Butler, Mr. Crane):** The true obstacle. Loyal to the *memory* of the elder Ashworth brother, he is suspicious of Nell's arrival and vigilant in protecting the house's secrets, which he may partially know. **Plot & Structure:** - **Arc:** From transactional intrusion to reluctant connection to chosen vulnerability. The "window" motif evolves from literal (climbing) to metaphorical (opening a heart). - **Pacing:** Slow-burn romance interwound with a heist. Each chapter escalates both the intimacy and the danger of discovery. - **Setting:** Ashworth Grange, a crumbling but beautiful Georgian manor in the Yorkshire Dales, late autumn. The atmosphere is key: fog, frost, decaying grandeur, a library full of old books, an observatory tower. **Stylistic Choices:** - **Tone:** Gothic-tinged romance. Atmospheric, with rich sensory detail (the smell of old paper, beeswax, and damp earth; the sound of rain on glass). The romance is tender and intellectual, built on shared curiosity and quiet moments. - **Voice:** First-person, past tense. Nell’s narration is her primary tool for control in a situation where she has little. It should feel intimate, as if she’s confessing to the reader. - **Avoid:** Melodrama, instalove, a villain who is cartoonishly evil. The tension should come from moral ambiguity and emotional stakes, not mustache-twirling. Nell must be competent and proactive, not rescued. **Core Intentions:** 1. Make the reader feel the physical reality of entering through a window — the strain, the fear, the exhilaration. 2. Build a romance that feels earned through specific, small moments of connection (over a shared book, a telescope view, a practical kindness). 3. Use the mystery of the letters to slowly reveal the true darkness of the man Nell thought she was stealing from, making her mission morally complex. 4. End with a choice that redefines what "home" and "safety" mean — perhaps the front door is opened from the inside. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Lesson** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Opening:** *In media res*. Nell is halfway up a rose trellis in the dark, rain making the stone slick. She’s entering the home of a *different* client, establishing her skill and profession. We see her method: observation, timing, practical tools (gloves, a small kit, sturdy boots under her skirt). - **Flashback/Calm:** The next day, in her bleak Bloomsbury room, she is hired by a distraught gentlewoman. The task: retrieve a packet of love letters from Ashworth Grange, written by the client to the late elder Ashworth brother. They could ruin her if published. Payment will secure Nell’s independence for a year. - **Preparation:** Nell researches the house (via an old architectural guide), plans her approach. She learns the younger brother, a recluse, now lives there. She secures a position as a temporary librarian/cataloguer (forged references) to gain legitimate entry, planning to do the real work at night. - **Arrival:** She arrives at the isolated Grange. Descrip tion of its imposing, slightly neglected state. She meets the taciturn butler, Mr. Crane, whose eyes miss nothing. She is shown to her room — an attic chamber with a window that has a very useful view of the roofline. **Chapter 2: The House and Its Ghosts** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Settling In:** Nell begins her "work" in the vast, disordered library. The atmosphere is heavy with the presence of the dead brother — his hunting trophies on the wall, his taste in decor. Crane patrols the halls. - **First Window (Practice):** That night, she does a practice run. She exits her attic window onto the roof, locates the dead brother’s study (on the second floor, now locked). She notes the window latch type, the drop to the garden. It’s risky, doable. - **First Encounter:** Returning along the roof, she is startled by a light in the observatory tower. Julian Ashworth is there, looking through a telescope. He doesn’t see her, but she watches him — a solitary, absorbed figure. He seems harmless, at odds with the house’s oppressive legacy. - **Complication:** The next day, while cataloguing, she finds a hidden drawer in a desk. It’s empty, but *recently* cleaned. Someone else is looking for something here. Her mission just became less straightforward. **Chapter 3: The Astronomer** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Forced Proximity:** A storm rolls in, cutting off the roads. Nell is effectively trapped at the Grange. Julian, as host, is forced to be more present. Their first real conversations happen over books in the library — he is awkward but brilliant, passionate about botany and the cosmos. - **Shared Moment:** He shows her the observatory. The intimacy of the dark, the shared awe of looking at Jupiter’s moons. He speaks of his brother with a complicated mix of grief and relief. "He owned the world. I only wanted to observe it." - **Suspicion:** Crane witnesses their easy(ish) conversation and his suspicion deepens. He finds an excuse to search Nell’s attic room while she’s at dinner. She returns to find her things subtly disturbed. The clock is ticking. - **The Plan Advances:** That night, she makes her first real attempt on the study window. She gets it open, slips inside. The room is a shrine to the dead brother. She finds a locked strongbox in the desk. No letters yet. But as she leaves, she sees a light moving in the garden below — Crane, on a late patrol. **Chapter 4: The Letters and the Ledger** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Discovery:** In the library, Nell follows a hunch and finds not the letters, but a private ledger tucked inside a book on estate management. It details payments to "a fixer" and mentions "the Devon matter" — alluding to a scandal that ruined a family. This was the elder brother’s true nature: cruel, exploitative. - **Moral Shift:** Nell’s mission is no longer just a job. The letters aren’t just embarrassing; they are potential *evidence* of a man’s predatory behavior. Her client may be one of many victims. - **Connection Deepens:** She and Julian bond over a neglected plant in the conservatory. He speaks of his brother’s lack of care for living things. There’s a charged moment where their hands touch over a pot of soil. The line between her role and her feelings blurs dangerously. - **Second Window:** She returns to the study, strongbox in sight. This time, she picks the lock. Inside: not letters, but a packet of *photographs* — compromising, cruel, depicting several women. And a single, damning letter of blackmail. This is worse than she imagined. **Chapter 5: The Storm Inside** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Crisis of Conscience:** Nell is in agony. She has the evidence, but it’s not the specific letters she was hired for. Taking this could expose not just the elder brother’s villainy, but potentially ruin innocent people in the photographs. Her client’s letter is there, but so are others. - **Emotional Climax:** A second, fiercer storm hits. Power fails. In the candlelit library, Julian confesses he feels a profound guilt for inheriting a house built on his brother’s cruelties, which he is only beginning to uncover. He asks Nell, almost desperately, if she believes a place can be redeemed. Their foreheads almost touch in the dark. - **Discovery:** Crane, having found a jemmy in Nell’s room (a tool from her kit), confronts her in the hall. He accuses her of being a thief. Julian intervenes, defending her, creating a rift between the two men. Nell is now under open suspicion. - **Decision:** She must act. She decides to take only her client’s specific letter from the packet and leave the rest, hoping to minimize the damage. But as she prepares for her final window entry, she finds the study door unlocked… and Julian’s lamp inside. **Chapter 6: The Observer Observed** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Confrontation:** Julian is not angry. He is devastated. He found the strongbox days ago. He was waiting to see what *she* would do. He read the letters, saw the photographs. He knows she was hired. "You came through the window," he says, "to steal back a ghost’s shame." - **Truth-Telling:** Nell, cornered, tells him everything: her fall from society, her work, her client’s fear. She explains she only wanted the one letter. Julian reveals his own truth: he’s been trying to find a way to make restitution to the victims, but doesn’t know how. He feels paralyzed by the legacy. - **Shift in Mission:** They form a wary alliance. The goal is no longer theft, but managed exposure. They will retrieve the entire packet of evidence together, with a plan to anonymously return the personal letters to the women, while using the financial records to force the family solicitor to create a compensation fund. - **The Final Window:** Together, they go to the study. But Crane, who has listened at the door, locks them in. He declares he will protect the family name from this "conspiracy." They are trapped, and the evidence is now in Crane’s hands. **Chapter 7: The Open Door** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Escape:** Using Nell’s tools and Julian’s knowledge of the house’s old servant passages, they escape the study. The chase through the dark, quiet house is tense but cooperative — a physical metaphor for their partnership. - **Confrontation with Crane:** They corner Crane in the great hall. Julian, finding his voice, delivers a quiet, devastating speech about what true loyalty means — not to a dead man’s sins, but to justice and the living. He dismisses Crane on the spot. - **The Choice:** With the packet recovered, Nell holds her client’s letter. She could simply take it and leave, completing her contract. But she looks at Julian, at the evidence of other ruined lives. She makes a choice. She puts the entire packet on the table. "We do this properly. All of it." - **Symbolic Action:** As dawn breaks, Julian doesn’t go to his telescope. He goes to the front door, unlocks it, and throws it wide open to the cold, clean morning air. "No more secrets in the dark," he says. **Chapter 8: The Morning Light** *Word count target: ~1000* - **Aftermath:** Weeks later. Nell is back in London, having forwarded the evidence to a trusted solicitor. Her client’s letter was returned; the woman is safe. A quiet financial settlement has been arranged for others. The scandal is contained, but the poison is drained. - **Return:** She receives a simple, botanical drawing from Julian — a rare Yorkshire flower, with a note: *"It blooms in harsh conditions. I should like to show you where. The front door is open. — J.A."* - **Final Scene:** She arrives at Ashworth Grange. It is transformed — Crane gone, windows cleaned, the oppressive atmosphere lifting. Julian greets her not in the library or observatory, but in the garden. He shows her the flower. They talk not of windows or walls, but of seasons, growth, and future plans for the estate. - **Closing Image:** That evening, she walks up the stairs to her old attic room. She opens the window — not to climb out, but to let the night air in. She looks out at the familiar roofline, now just a view, not a path. She closes it gently and goes back downstairs, where light and conversation wait. The art of entering has been forgotten; the art of staying has just begun.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
This is an excellent exercise. Writing with "humanness" means embracing imperfection, specificity, and emotional risk—areas where default LLM behavior often retreats into pattern-matching and safety. ### **How a Human Might Approach This Prompt** 1. **Start with a Specific Sensation:** A human writer might begin not with plot, but with a single, visceral image from the prompt: *the feeling of cold stone under fingertips at 2 a.m., the burn in the forearms, the terrifying slip of a boot on wet ivy.* They'd chase the physical, sensory truth of the act first, and let the story grow from that seed. 2. **Embrace an Unreliable or Flawed Lens:** The narrator wouldn't be a perfectly competent, morally clear operative. She'd be frightened, make mistakes, rationalize her actions, and feel contradictory things (e.g., a thrill alongside the shame). Her voice would have specific, odd tics—not just "wry and observational." 3. **Find the Unexpected Angles:** A human might obsess over logistical weirdness: *What skirt allows climbing? How does one explain muddy gloves? What if the gardener sees and thinks it's a ghost?* The romance might spark not over stars, but over an awkward, shared moment of practical problem-solving (e.g., both reaching for the same dropped tool). 4. **Prioritize Subtext Over Melodrama:** The tension would live in what's *not* said. The human writer would ask: What is the one topic they avoid? What small gesture carries huge meaning? The conflict wouldn't be a shouting match but a carefully phrased question that lands like a blow. 5. **Leave Things Unresolved or Messy:** A human ending might not tie every thread. Perhaps the client's full story remains ambiguous, or the future of the relationship is hopeful but uncertain. The moral compromise might not feel clean. ### **Common LLM Writing Pitfalls (and Why They Fail Here)** * **The "Competence Porn" Trap:** Over-emphasizing the protagonist's flawless skill (the "art" in the prompt) can make her feel like a superhero, not a person. Danger is neutered if she's never truly scared or clumsy. * **The "Aesthetic Wallpaper" Problem:** Lavishing excessive, generic description on gothic settings (e.g., "the brooding manor loomed beneath a sapphire sky") that serves tone but not character or plot. It's *telling* atmosphere, not *earning* it through action and detail. * **The "Romance Beat Sheet" Fallback:** Hitting prescribed romance milestones (meet-cute, banter, forced proximity, almost-kiss, misunderstanding, grand gesture) in a predictable sequence, sacrificing organic development for formula. * **The "Moral Clarity" Imperative:** LLMs often instinctively make characters' motivations purely sympathetic and resolutions morally neat. The protagonist can't be *too* compromised; the love interest must be *wholly* good; the villain must be *unambiguously* bad. This robs stories of real tension. * **The "Bow-Wrapped" Resolution:** An overwhelming drive to provide closure and catharsis for every character and plot thread, often with a hopeful, future-oriented final line. Real human stories often end on a note of poignant uncertainty. * **The "Lexical Showing Off" Tendency:** Using complex words or ornate sentence structures not because the character would think that way, but to signal "this is literary." It breaks voice and distances the reader. ### **Deep Critique of the Current Plan: Where It Might Fall Into LLM Tropes** 1. **Over-Competent Protagonist:** Nell's skills (forging references, architectural research, lock-picking, roof navigation) are comprehensive. The plan notes her competence but doesn't deeply explore her fear or fallibility. *Risk: She becomes a plot device to execute the heist, not a vulnerable person we fear for.* 2. **The "Secretly Noble" Love Interest:** Julian is designed to be instantly sympathetic (reclusive scholar, guilt-ridden, gentle with plants). His complicity in his brother's legacy is passive. The moral heavy lifting is done by the dead brother and Crane. *Risk: Julian feels like a reward for Nell's suffering, not a complex individual with his own potential for moral failure.* 3. **Clear Moral Antagonists:** The elder brother (cartoonishly evil from the grave) and Crane (the rigid traditionalist) create a simple "good vs. bad legacy" dynamic. *Risk: This simplifies the ethical landscape. What if Julian's hands aren't entirely clean? What if Crane's loyalty has a more understandable, tragic root?* 4. **The "Alliance of the Righteous" Climax:** In Ch. 6, Nell and Julian quickly unite against a common, clearer evil (the brother's sins, Crane's obstruction). *Risk: This bypasses the more interesting, human conflict: What if they fundamentally disagree on what to do with the evidence? What if Julian's primary impulse is to protect his family name, not seek justice?* 5. **The Neat, Symbolic Resolution:** The front door thrown open is a powerful symbol, but it risks being *too* perfect, too literary. The dismissal of Crane is swift and clean. The financial settlement is arranged off-page. *Risk: The messy, protracted, uncertain real-world work of justice is skipped for a satisfying emotional beat.* 6. **The Epilogue Tie-Everything-Up Bow:** Ch. 8 provides closure on the legal matter, the client's safety, the romance, and the transformation of the house. *Risk: It feels like a "where are they now" card, sacrificing the lingering resonance of ambiguity for completeness.* ### **Brainstorming Ideas to Make It More Human** * **Give Nell a Contradictory, Specific Flaw:** Maybe she's a kleptomaniac who steals small, meaningless objects (a button, a thimble) from every house she enters, compulsively. This adds psychological depth and a real risk of discovery unrelated to the main mission. * **Complicate Julian:** What if he *did* know about his brother's predatory behavior, but chose to look away to maintain his peace and funding for his scientific work? His guilt isn't passive; it's active complicity. His connection with Nell forces him to confront this. * **Make Crane's Loyalty Personal & Tragic:** Perhaps the elder brother saved Crane or his family from ruin years ago. His vigilance isn't just about duty; it's about repaying a debt that now feels morally rotten. This makes him a tragic figure, not just an obstacle. * **Let the "Art" Be Messy:** Have one of her window entries go badly wrong—she's nearly caught, drops a crucial tool, injures herself (a sprained wrist, a deep cut). The physical consequence raises stakes and shows cost. * **Focus on a Specific, Un-Gothic Detail:** Instead of generic "decaying grandeur," have Nell fixate on one odd detail: a single, expensive French wallpaper panel peeling in the study, revealing older, plainer paper beneath. It becomes a metaphor for the house's secrets. * **Subvert the Romantic Climax:** Their first true moment of intimacy isn't a kiss or a confession, but a silent, shared act of practical deception—lying together to Crane about why they're both in the library at midnight. It's a partnership born in transgression. * **Embrace an Ambiguous, Not-Neat Ending:** Maybe she returns the letters, but the client, now safe, refuses to speak to her. Maybe Julian, after the truth is out, must leave the Grange to make amends in London, and their future is a question mark. The house isn't magically cleansed; it's just quietly, painfully changed. * **Give the Prose a Specific, Slightly Odd Voice:** Perhaps Nell's narration is overly precise about certain things (mechanisms, distances, plant names—things she learned from her father) and vague or evasive about others (her emotions, her past). The voice itself is a character with tells. The goal is to **shift the focus from executing a pre-plotted arc to discovering the story through the character's specific, flawed, sensory experience.** It means valuing a single, strange, true detail over ten lines of competent description, and letting characters make choices that are surprising, messy, and humanly understandable, even if they aren't cleanly "good."
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **The Story in a Nutshell:** A woman who fell from grace has found a grim, specific niche: retrieving compromising letters for blackmail victims. She is hired to enter a remote Yorkshire manor—through an upper window—and retrieve a set of letters. She expects to find the villainous owner. Instead, she finds his quiet, scholarly younger brother, who has inherited the house and its dark legacy. The story is about the collision of two people defined by a dead man's sins: one who enters through windows to survive, and one who inherited a house he can't bear to inhabit properly. **Character & Voice:** - **Protagonist (Nell):** Late 20s. Daughter of a bankrupt clergyman. Competent but not superhuman—she is often cold, sometimes frightened, and carries a quiet shame. She narrates in a close first-person that is precise about physical detail (distances, mechanisms, the geometry of climbing) but evasive about emotion. Her voice is the voice of someone trained to observe everything outside herself to avoid feeling what's inside. - **Love Interest (Julian):** Early 30s. A botanist, not a romantic hero. He is awkward, possibly on the spectrum, and has a habit of stating uncomfortable truths plainly. He is not brooding—he is *avoidant*. He inherited his brother's house and money but has done nothing to confront what his brother was. He is not secretly noble; he is complicit through inaction, and he knows it. - **Butler (Crane):** Not a villain. A man in his 60s who owes his livelihood—and possibly his life—to the elder Ashworth. He is protecting a debt of loyalty that has curdled into something corrosive. He is pitiable, which makes him more dangerous. **Plot & Structure:** - **Arc:** Nell enters as a professional intruder. She discovers the situation is morally more complex than she was told. She must decide: complete the job as contracted, or intervene in a larger wrong. The romance complicates this further. - **Central Tension:** Not "will she get caught?" but "what is she willing to risk, and for whom?" - **Setting:** Ashworth Grange, Yorkshire Dales, November. The house is not a gothic cliché—it is a well-built Georgian manor slowly losing a battle with damp, neglected by a man who would rather be elsewhere. **Stylistic Choices:** - **Tone:** Understated, physical, cold. The romance is built in silence and small practical kindnesses, not declarations. The gothic element comes from the *situation*—secrets, a dead man's power, the winter isolation—not from purple prose. - **Voice:** First-person, present-tense for climbing/window scenes (immediacy, danger), past-tense for everything else (reflection, distance). This shift signals when Nell is in her body versus in her head. - **What I'm Avoiding:** Instalove. Moral clarity. A villain. A neat ending. Competence porn. Ornate description that serves no one. The protagonist being rescued. The romance "fixing" either character. **Core Intentions:** 1. **Serve the prompt above all.** The phrase "one quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows" is the engine. Every chapter should honor the *physical reality* of this—the skill, the risk, the specific knowledge it requires. 2. **Let characters be inconvenient.** Julian should not be easy to like at first. Nell should not be easy to trust. Their connection should be surprising to them both. 3. **Earn the emotional beats.** No moment of intimacy should arrive without specific, accumulated detail. A shared silence should carry as much weight as a confession. 4. **End on a question, not an answer.** The story should close with possibility, not resolution. The final image should echo the first, but transformed. --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: The Art (800 words)** - **Open *in media res*:** Nell halfway up a wall in Bloomsbury. Rain. Her fingers ache. She thinks about distance to the window ledge—three feet, maybe less. She makes it. Inside, she retrieves a letter from a desk. She leaves by the front door, unlocked from the inside. *This is how it works.* - **Brief scene:** Next morning. A teahouse. Her client, a Mrs. Haverford, hires her for the real job: Ashworth Grange. The letters are from a love affair with the elder Mr. Ashworth, now dead. The younger brother has the house. Mrs. Haverford is vague about why the letters are dangerous. Nell notices she doesn't cry—she is *afraid.* - **Arrival that evening:** The Grange is smaller than she expected. Plain. The butler, Crane, watches her from the doorway as the trap drives away. He shows her to an attic room. The window looks out over a gentle roof pitch and a drainpipe. She notes: *useful.* **Chapter 2: The House (900 words)** - **Daytime work:** Cataloguing the library. The elder Ashworth's books are expensive and unread. Julian's are dog-eared, full of pressed leaves. She finds evidence someone else has searched this room recently—books replaced out of order, a desk drawer wiped clean. - **Night practice run:** She exits through her window. The roof is slick with frost, not rain—harder. She locates the dead brother's study: second floor, east wing, window latched but old. She notes the mechanism. On her way back, she sees a light in the tower room. A man at a telescope. He doesn't look down. - **Next morning:** She meets Julian properly in the library. He is not charming. He asks what she thinks of the cataloguing system, listens to her answer, says "That's wrong," and explains why. He leaves. She is unsettled—not by rudeness, but by the fact he actually cared about her answer. **Chapter 3: The Cold (1000 words)** - **Storm isolates them.** Roads flooded. Nell is trapped at the Grange for days. Julian, forced to host, is visibly uncomfortable. They eat in near-silence. - **A small moment:** She finds him in the conservatory trying to save a frost-damaged orchid. She knows plants—her father's vicarage had a garden. She suggests a different soil mix. He tries it. They don't speak of anything else. Later, the orchid improves. He leaves a note on her door: *Thank you.* - **First real conversation:** In the library, evening. He tells her, flatly, that his brother was "not a good man." He doesn't elaborate. He asks nothing of her. She feels, uncomfortably, that he is telling her something important and also asking nothing because he is afraid of the answer. - **That night:** She enters the study through the window. The latch is stiff; she works it slowly, terrified of noise. Inside: a locked strongbox. No time to pick it. She leaves. In the garden below, she sees Crane's lantern moving. He is patrolling. He stops beneath the study window and looks up. **Chapter 4: The Strongbox (1000 words)** - **Nell searches the library** for a key or clue. Instead, she finds a ledger hidden in a book on drainage. It records payments—large sums—to unnamed recipients. "For the Devon arrangement." "For the Bristol matter." The elder Ashworth was paying people off. For what? - **She and Julian** are thrown together more. He shows her the observatory—his refuge. He explains planetary motion with his hands, awkwardly, beautifully. She feels a pull she doesn't want. In the dark, he says: "I should have looked into the accounts before now. I didn't want to know." - **Second window entry:** She picks the strongbox. Inside: a packet of letters and photographs. The letters are blackmail—demands for money, threats of exposure. The photographs are of women, some of whom Nell recognizes from society. This is not a love affair. It is a *catalogue of victims.* - **One letter is in a different hand**—a plea, not a demand. It's from Mrs. Haverford. It's not a love letter. It's a letter begging Ashworth to stop, to return the photographs he took of her. Nell's client didn't have an affair. She was preyed upon. She hired Nell not to retrieve love letters, but *evidence.* **Chapter 5: The Weight (1000 words)** - **Nell is in turmoil.** She has the evidence. Taking just her client's letter feels insufficient—there are other women here. Taking everything could blow the matter wide in ways she can't control. - **Julian finds her in the library** at an odd hour. He sees her face. He doesn't ask what's wrong. He makes tea. They sit in silence. Then he says: "Whatever you found, you should know—I have decided to do nothing with this house. Sell it. Walk away. That is my plan." She hears cowardice. She also hears exhaustion. - **Crane confronts her.** He doesn't accuse her of theft—he asks, politely, why she was in the east wing at night. She lies. He knows she lies. He says: "I have served this family for forty years. I know what I owe." His loyalty is not to a person—it is to a *debt.* - **That night, she decides:** She will take the whole packet. All of it. She will find a way to return the personal items to each woman anonymously, and use the financial records to force the family solicitor to make restitution. It's messy. It's beyond her contract. She does it anyway. - **She goes to the study window.** It's unlocked. Inside, Julian is sitting at the desk. The strongbox is open. He looks at her. "How long have you been climbing through my windows?" he asks. Not angry. Just tired. **Chapter 6: The Debt (1000 words)** - **The confrontation is quiet.** He tells her he found the strongbox a week ago. He read some of it. He couldn't read all of it. He put it back and locked it and tried to forget. He says: "I was waiting to see what you would do." - **She tells him everything**—her fall, her work, Mrs. Haverford's real story. She does not ask for forgiveness. He does not offer it. He says: "I inherited this. I didn't cause it. But I haven't stopped it either, have I? Sitting in this house, using his money. That's its own kind of—" He doesn't finish. - **They form no heroic alliance.** They simply agree on one thing: the women in those photographs deserve to have their property returned. The rest—the justice, the consequences—is beyond them. They will return the personal letters. The financial evidence they will leave with the family solicitor, anonymously, and let the legal system do what it will. - **Crane locks them in the study.** He has been listening. He says he will protect the family. Julian, for the first time, speaks with quiet authority: "Whose family, Crane? His? Or mine?" **Chapter 7: The Return (1000 words)** - **They escape through servant passages** Nell didn't know about—Julian knows the house better than he admits. The sequence is tense, physical, dark. They move through the house like ghosts. - **Confrontation with Crane in the hall.** It is not dramatic. Julian tells him he is dismissed—with a generous pension, with reference, with everything that acknowledges the debt. Crane looks at Nell and says: "You came through the window." It is not an accusation. It is an observation. He leaves in the morning. - **Nell packs her bag.** She takes the packet. Julian walks her to the door. It is 5 a.m., dark, freezing. He says: "You could stay. You could come back. Through the front door." She doesn't answer. She doesn't know the answer. - **She walks down the drive.** She looks back once. He is still in the doorway, lit from behind. She turns away. **Chapter 8: The View (800 words)** - **Six weeks later, London.** The letters have been returned. Mrs. Haverford sent a single line: *Thank you.* No meeting, no gratitude. That is how it works. - **A letter from Julian:** Not romantic. A botanical drawing of a frost orchid, with a note in his plain hand: *It survived. The garden is beginning. If you are ever in Yorkshire, the door is open. I mean the front one.* - **Final scene:** She doesn't go to Yorkshire. Not yet. But she thinks about it. She thinks about the house, and the window, and the man who said *the door is open.* She is in her London room, and she opens the window—not to climb out, just for air. The city sounds come in. She lets them. - **Last line:** She closes the window. Not locked. Just closed.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Eleanor "Nell" Carroway **Background & Personality:** Daughter of a Cambridgeshire clergyman who died leaving significant debts. Her mother had died years prior. She had one season in London at 19, modest and promising, before her father's financial ruin became public. The engagement she thought she had dissolved overnight. She spent two years as a governess to a merchant family in Leeds, where she learned to be invisible and indispensable, before a chance encounter with a woman in similar straits led to her current work. She has been retrieving letters and compromising items for three years. She is not proud of it. She is not ashamed of it. She is *used* to it, which is its own kind of problem. She is precise in her habits. She folds her clothes in specific ways. She counts steps on staircases. She notices door hinges—which side they swing from, whether they squeak. These are survival mechanisms that have calcified into personality. She has a dry, internal humor she almost never voices. She is capable of warmth but has trained herself to withhold it, because warmth is a liability in her work. She genuinely likes plants and misses her father's garden more than she misses her father, who was kind but ineffectual. **Specific Details:** - She bites the inside of her cheek when concentrating—has scar tissue there. - She always wears a plain silver ring on her right hand, third finger. It was her mother's. It's the only thing of value she owns and she has never sold it, though she has considered it twice. - She knows an unsettling amount about lock mechanisms, roof pitches, and the structural weaknesses of Victorian construction. - She reads novels but only ones she's read before. She doesn't like surprises in fiction; she gets enough of them in life. - She makes tea well and judges people by how they make tea. Julian makes terrible tea. She finds this oddly endearing. **Physicality:** Nell is 5'6", neither striking nor plain—she is the kind of woman who can be overlooked, and she has cultivated this. Dark hair she keeps pinned tightly. A narrow face with a sharp jaw. Her hands are her most expressive feature: long-fingered, strong, always in motion—fidgeting with a button, tracing the edge of a table. She moves quietly and deliberately, a habit from her work. When she's not performing a role (the cataloguer, the unremarkable woman), her posture is slightly hunched, protective. When she climbs, she is entirely transformed—focused, athletic, almost graceful. That is when she looks most like herself, though she would not say so. **Motives & Allegiances:** - **Primary motive:** Financial survival and, beneath it, the faint hope of enough money to stop. To live somewhere small and quiet where she doesn't have to enter through windows. - **Allegiance:** To her clients, professionally. To herself, primarily. She has learned not to form allegiances—they compromise the work. - **Existing relationships:** A few former clients who might speak well of her, though none socially. A landlady in Bloomsbury who thinks she is a secretary. No close friends. This is deliberate. - **Relationship to Julian:** Initially: he is a complication, an obstacle to be navigated.
